Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually dedicating about 3-4 hours most days to my coursework. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time making notes and rewriting concepts in my own words. Honestly though, with 13 online courses, it was hard to give each one the attention it deserved. I'd block out time in my planner, but sometimes anxiety would creep in and I'd end up just staring at my notes without really absorbing much.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied more than I'd like to admit. I tried to study at regular times, but when deadlines piled up or my anxiety spiked, everything went out the window. Some weeks I'd be really disciplined, and other weeks I'd be scrambling at the last minute. The inconsistency bothered me because I know I work better with structure, but life kept getting in the way.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I remembered an assignment was coming up or when I got a notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because my anxiety made me paranoid about deadlines. It wasn't really a habit so much as a reaction to feeling like I might be falling behind. Occasionally I'd log in after peer support sessions when someone mentioned something I hadn't seen.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more automatic, especially at the start of the semester when I was motivated to stay on top of things. But honestly, it never really stuck as a true routine for me. The LMS felt more like a tool I used when needed rather than a daily habit. When my anxiety was manageable, I'd check in more regularly, but those moments were inconsistent.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were definitely most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I appreciated when professors posted detailed notes or articles I could download and annotate. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in ways that clicked for me. Videos were okay, but I found myself preferring to read transcripts when they were available.
Q6: I often skipped the video content or only watched parts of it because I retain information better through reading. The peer interaction features felt awkward to me, so I only used them minimally. Some of the optional practice quizzes I'd ignore when I was overwhelmed, even though I knew they'd probably help. When my anxiety was high, anything that felt like extra work got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If I could tell it would help me understand something I was struggling with, I'd engage with it fully. Clear instructions and knowing exactly what was expected made a huge difference. When things felt vague or disconnected from my grades, my motivation to complete them dropped significantly.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford with everything else on my plate. Required work got prioritized because of the grade pressure, even when optional stuff might have been more interesting. There was this constant mental calculation of 'will this help my grade directly?' and if the answer was no, it went to the bottom of my list. Sometimes I felt guilty about skipping optional things, which just added to my anxiety.
Q9: Absolutely, I did so much studying offline that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download PDFs and readings, then work through them on my tablet without internet. I kept a physical notebook where I rewrote key concepts and made my own study guides. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed materials spread across my desk, not logged into anything.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading downloaded articles without any LMS activity. I'd sometimes watch a video once online, then replay concepts in my head while reviewing my written notes later. Group study sessions with peers happened in person or over text, not through the platform. My best studying often happened when I was completely disconnected from the computer.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my preferred method. I'd access a reading or resource once, download it or take screenshots, and then study it offline multiple times. It helped reduce my anxiety to not have to be connected all the time. I'd revisit my downloaded materials repeatedly before exams, but the LMS would only show that single initial access. This was especially true for the longer readings that I'd work through over several days.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety made it really hard to engage. When I felt overwhelmed by the sheer number of courses and deadlines, I'd kind of freeze up. Sometimes I'd log in, see all the tasks, and just feel paralyzed. The peer support intervention actually helped during one of those rough patches, which surprised me because I usually prefer working alone.
Q13: The workload across 13 courses was genuinely overwhelming at times. When I was exhausted or confused about material, I'd avoid the LMS because logging in just reminded me of everything I wasn't getting done. Competing responsibilities like part-time work and family stuff meant some weeks the LMS barely got opened. Fatigue especially affected my ability to engage meaningfully with anything.
Q14: Definitely. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials but my mind was somewhere else entirely. My anxiety would have me checking and rechecking things without actually processing the information. There were times I'd watch videos while my brain was just static, not retaining anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. My anxiety sometimes made starting feel impossible, like this wall I couldn't climb over. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but that better day didn't always come. The irony is that avoiding things made my anxiety worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like the only option.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd have bursts of intense activity right before things were due, which probably looked chaotic from a data perspective. Without a deadline looming, I struggled to motivate myself to engage with materials. It's not ideal, but that pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and get me moving.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, sometimes in unexpected ways. Positive feedback gave me a boost and made me want to engage more with that course. But critical feedback, even when constructive, could send my anxiety spiraling and make me avoid that course for a while. I appreciated detailed feedback the most because it gave me something concrete to work with.
Q18: The course structure mattered a lot for my ability to participate. When things were clearly laid out with explicit deadlines and expectations, I could manage my anxiety better. Courses with vague structures or constantly changing requirements were really difficult for me. I did better when I could see the whole semester mapped out and plan accordingly.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement much easier for me as a read-write learner. What made it harder was when courses relied heavily on video content without written alternatives. Discussion requirements felt forced and uncomfortable given my anxiety about peer interactions. Clear, written instructions for assignments were always helpful, while ambiguous prompts increased my stress significantly.
Q20: I think that pattern could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking things obsessively without actually completing work. They might be trying to engage but getting stuck in a loop of preparation without action. It could also be someone who's struggling to understand the material and keeps reviewing without feeling ready to submit anything. Fear of failure might keep them browsing but not committing to submissions.
Q21: This actually resonates with my experience somewhat. I did a lot of my real learning offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. My exam score was decent even though my LMS engagement looked low because the learning was happening elsewhere. Someone could appear disengaged online but be deeply engaged with the material in ways the system can't track.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, which gives some picture of behavior. But they miss the quality of engagement and whether you're actually learning or just going through motions. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or the depth of understanding. My anxiety-driven checking behavior might look like engagement when it's really just stress.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious and thinking about concepts even outside of study time. For me, real engagement is when I'm making connections and the material starts to make sense in a deeper way. It's more about mental investment than time spent logged into a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with printed and handwritten materials, which is where my real learning happened. It can't capture the peer support conversations that actually helped me push through difficult moments. My anxiety and the mental energy I spent just managing it to be able to study at all is completely invisible. The system sees my low engagement numbers but not the genuine effort behind my passing grade.
